reasoning: The Fed is clearly in an easing cycle, with the effective federal fund rate (EFFR) dropping to 4.120 by October 2025 from its peak of 5.33% in 2023-2024. EFFR is highly stable and tightly maintained by the Fed between FOMC meetings, meaning short-term random increases (ticks up) are quite rare — most days it remains perfectly flat to the basis point. For the near-term dates (2025-11-16 and 2025-12-09), there are unlikely to be any scheduled FOMC rate hikes, and random drifts upward are constrained by Fed tools like the Standing Repo Facility. Thus, the probability of an increase over this short window is extremely low. For the longer-term date (2026-02-07), one or two additional FOMC meetings will have occurred (mid-December, late January). In an ongoing easing cycle, these meetings are vastly more likely to yield further cuts or holds rather than hikes. However, uncertainty around inflation resurgence or economic conditions naturally increases the longer the horizon, so the probability of an eventual rate increase is assigned higher weight over time, yet remains broadly unlikely.
